text: The surgeon places five ports, including one for the camera, one for the liver retractor, and three working ports. The liver is lifted off the stomach to expose the esophagus-stomach junction. The connective tissue overlying this region is carefully divided using an ultrasonic dissector, which vibrates to cut and seal tissue, minimizing thermal damage to surrounding structures.

The surgeon completes the dissection and enters the chest, using gentle blunt dissection to expose the esophagus. Loose tissues are divided with a sweeping motion, and vessels are sealed with a coagulator. The instrument tip is cooled on the liver to prevent injury. The fat pad over the gastroesophageal junction is divided, raising the vagus nerve off the esophagus. An eight-centimeter umbilical tape measures the myotomy length, extending six centimeters into the esophagus and two centimeters into the stomach. The stomach is marked with electrocautery, and a dilator is placed into the esophagus. The myotomy begins on the anterior border of the esophagus, using an ultrasonic dissector to lift muscle layers off the inner mucosal layer, preventing injury and limiting bleeding.

The surgeon continues the myotomy, dividing the muscle layers of the esophagus and extending the dissection into the anterior wall of the stomach. The electrocautery is used to carefully separate the longitudinal, circular, and diagonal muscle fibers, ensuring the underlying mucosa is not injured. The deepest diagonal fibers are lifted off the mucosa and cut with the cautery. After completing the myotomy, the mucosa is inspected for residual muscle fibers and potential injuries, with sutures placed to prevent complications.

The video endoscope illuminates the inside of the esophagus, allowing the surgeon to assess for mucosal leaks and ensure complete division of muscle fibers. The endoscope helps confirm that the myotomy extends sufficiently into the stomach, with internal markings aiding in precise identification.

The surgeon creates an anterior wrap to prevent reflux by dividing the lateral attachments and blood vessels to mobilize the stomach. Sutures are placed from the stomach through the cut edge of the muscle and tacked to the diaphragm, covering the exposed mucosa and preventing muscle scarring. Three sutures attach the wrap to the left diaphragm, followed by three more to the right diaphragm, completing the wrap and covering the esophageal mucosa.

The surgeon places and ties the final sutures, completing the anterior wrap where the stomach is positioned in front of the esophagus and myotomy, and attached to the diaphragm. This configuration visually forms a one-way valve intended to reduce acid reflux into the esophagus and minimize postoperative indigestion.
